topics.title: CompletableFuture & Async Programming
topics.description: CompletableFuture (Java 8+) enables non-blocking async programming with a fluent pipeline API. Unlike Future (which blocks on .get()), CompletableFuture allows chaining: thenApply (transform), thenAccept (consume), thenCompose (flat-map async), thenCombine (merge two async results). Error handling: exceptionally, handle. Execution: runs on ForkJoinPool.commonPool() by default, or your own Executor. Key for building reactive-style pipelines without reactive frameworks.
topics.story: Old Future: 'I'll do this task and you have to wait at my door until I'm done (blocking .get()).' CompletableFuture: 'I'll do this task. When done, I'll call you with the result (callback). While waiting, you can do other things.' This is the difference between a phone call you have to hold for (blocking) vs a callback — 'press 1 when ready, we'll call you back'. Composing multiple async calls: fetch user, then fetch their orders, then calculate total — all without blocking threads.
topics.analogy: CompletableFuture = an order ticket at a restaurant. You place the order (start async task), get a ticket (CompletableFuture), and can specify what to do when the food arrives (thenApply/thenAccept). thenCompose = 'when the appetizer arrives, start cooking the main course' (sequential async). thenCombine = 'wait for both the wine delivery AND the food, then serve together' (parallel async merge). exceptionally = 'if food burns, serve a sandwich instead'.
topics.memoryAnchor: supplyAsync(supplier) → async computation returns value. runAsync(runnable) → async, no return. thenApply(fn) → transform (like map). thenAccept(fn) → consume, returns void. thenCompose(fn) → flat-map (fn returns CompletableFuture). thenCombine(other, fn) → merge two CFs. allOf(cfs) → wait for all. anyOf(cfs) → first to finish. exceptionally(fn) → handle error, provide default. handle(fn) → both success and error paths. join() blocks (no checked exception). get() blocks (throws checked).
topics.firstPrinciples: Traditional thread-blocking I/O wastes a thread while waiting. Async I/O releases the thread during wait, reuses it for other work. CompletableFuture wraps async computation in a monad — transformations (thenApply) don't execute immediately but build a lazy pipeline that fires when the upstream completes. This is the Java approach to async/await patterns.
topics.bruteForce: ExecutorService + Future: Future<String> f = executor.submit(() -> fetchUser(id)); String user = f.get(); // BLOCKS here. To run two tasks in parallel: submit both, then call .get() on both. Complex chaining requires manual callback hell or blocking at each step.
topics.optimizedApproach: CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> fetchUser(id)).thenApply(user -> transform(user)).thenAccept(result -> save(result)). For parallel: thenCombine runs second task while first is running. allOf waits for all without blocking a thread until the final .join().
topics.whenToUse: CompletableFuture: calling multiple independent external APIs in parallel, building async pipelines, non-blocking service calls. Don't use for CPU-heavy tasks on commonPool (can starve other tasks — use custom Executor). For truly reactive programming: Spring WebFlux + Project Reactor (Mono/Flux). For simple parallel batch: parallelStream() or structured concurrency (Java 21).

examples.title: Async Pipeline — thenApply, thenCompose, exceptionally
examples.explanation: thenCompose vs thenApply: thenApply transforms the value (like map), thenCompose chains another async operation (like flatMap — use when the transformation itself is async). allOf runs all CFs concurrently, .join() at the end waits without blocking a thread during the wait. exceptionally provides a fallback value on any upstream failure.
examples.realWorldUse: Parallel API calls: fetch user profile + permissions + preferences simultaneously with allOf, combine all three. Spring @Async returns CompletableFuture. Microservices: aggregate response from 3 downstream services in parallel instead of sequential. Email+SMS notification: both sent async, main thread continues. WebClient (Spring WebFlux) returns Mono<T> — similar concept.

examples.title: thenCombine, handle & Custom Executor
examples.explanation: thenCombine runs both CFs concurrently and merges results when BOTH complete. handle receives both result and exception (one will be null) — unlike exceptionally which only handles errors. Custom Executor prevents CPU-intensive tasks from saturating ForkJoinPool.commonPool(). whenComplete adds side effects (logging, metrics) without altering the pipeline result.
examples.realWorldUse: Price aggregator: fetch price from 3 vendors in parallel, thenCombine to find minimum. handle for circuit-breaker fallback. Custom IO_POOL for database or HTTP calls — prevents commonPool starvation. Log all async operation completions with whenComplete for observability.

problems.title: Parallel API Aggregation
problems.description: Simulate fetching data from 3 'microservices' in parallel: getUsername(userId), getUserEmail(userId), getUserAge(userId) — each takes 200ms. Use CompletableFuture to run all three simultaneously and combine results into a Map<String,Object>. Print total time taken.
problems.hint1: Start all 3 CFs immediately (don't chain sequentially).
problems.hint2: allOf(cf1, cf2, cf3).join() waits for all to complete.
problems.hint3: After allOf, call .join() on each individual CF to get its result.

problems.title: Async with Timeout and Fallback
problems.description: Write an async operation that fetches data but times out after 150ms. If it times out or fails, return a fallback value. Use completeOnTimeout (Java 9+) or orTimeout.
problems.hint1: cf.completeOnTimeout(fallback, 150, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS) completes with fallback if not done in time.
problems.hint3: Chain .exceptionally after orTimeout to provide fallback.
problems.testCases:
Fast: real data
Slow: fallback data
orTimeout: cached: fallback

problems.title: Sequential vs Parallel Time Comparison
problems.description: Write two versions of fetching data from 4 'services' (each taking 100ms): (1) sequential using .thenCompose chain, (2) parallel using allOf. Measure and print the time for each. The parallel version should take ~100ms vs ~400ms sequential.
problems.hint2: Parallel: start all 4 CFs immediately, then allOf.join().
problems.hint3: Use System.currentTimeMillis() before and after .join() to measure.
